with_agent_vs_baseline.pass_rate: +28% (100% vs 72%) — baseline makes correct decisions but uses non-standard schema
with_agent_vs_baseline.tokens: +14% more tokens (17K vs 15K — reads instruction file)
with_agent_vs_baseline.duration: +31% slower (12.4s vs 9.5s — reads instruction file but still fast)
with_agent_vs_baseline.quality: Both configs make 100% correct GO/STOP decisions. The only failures are schema compliance — baseline outputs_valid_json always fails (missing next_phase, suppliers_to_skip, estimated_value). Decision quality is identical. For a simple classification agent like phase-gater, the agent instruction overhead is minimal (~3s) and schema compliance matters for orchestrator consumption.

notes: Decision correctness is 100% for BOTH configs — all GO/STOP decisions correct across all 3 evals
The ONLY discriminating assertion is outputs_valid_json — always passes with agent, always fails without
Content assertions (decides_go, decides_stop, rationale quality, supplier lists) pass in both configs
Phase-gater is the fastest agent: ~12s with agent, ~10s without. Haiku handles this well
Baseline RPVS run had 0 tool uses — Haiku without instructions didn't even write the file (had to be manually created)
Both configs complete under 15s — well within the overhead budget for a gate decision
The agent adds schema compliance (next_phase, suppliers_to_skip, estimated_value) at minimal cost (+3s)
